cards.concept: The key concepts introduced in Chapter 14
cards.explanation:
Chapter 14 introduces object-oriented programming in C++:
  • Classes and member functions
  • Access specifiers (public/private) and encapsulation
  • Constructors (default, parameterized, copy, delegating)
  • Member initializer lists
  • Const member functions and const objects
  • Access functions (getters/setters)
  • Explicit keyword and converting constructors
  • Copy elision and RVO
  • Constexpr classes
examples: class MyClass { private: int m_data; public: int getData() const; };
MyClass() : m_data{0} { }  // constructor with initializer list

cards.text: The three key principles for good class design: Make data members {{c1::private}}, provide {{c2::public member functions}} for controlled access, and use {{c3::constructors}} to ensure valid initialization.

cards.front: What are the four main benefits of using classes over structs?
cards.back:
• Encapsulation - bundling data and functions together
  • Data hiding - private members prevent unauthorized access
  • Invariant enforcement - control how data is modified
  • Abstraction - separate interface from implementation

cards.question: Identify all the issues with this class design:
cards.code:
class BankAccount
{
public:
    double balance;
    void setBalance(double b) { balance = b; }
    double getBalance() { return balance; }
};
cards.answer:
• balance should be private, not public
  • getBalance() should be const
  • setBalance() should validate input
  • Should use m_ naming convention for members
  • Should have constructor to initialize balance
cards.explanation: Multiple best practice violations: public data, non-const getter, no validation, no constructor.

cards.front: What are the five types of constructors covered in Chapter 14?
cards.back:
• Default constructor (no parameters)
  • Parameterized constructor
  • Copy constructor
  • Delegating constructor
  • Converting constructor (single parameter, implicit conversion)

cards.text: The {{c1::Rule of Three}}: If you define a custom {{c2::destructor}}, {{c3::copy constructor}}, or {{c4::copy assignment operator}}, you should probably define all three.

cards.question: Write a complete, well-designed class for a simple Point in 2D space:
cards.code:
// Should have x, y coordinates
// Should be immutable after construction
// Should support compile-time evaluation
cards.answer:
class Point
{
private:
    int m_x{};
    int m_y{};
public:
    constexpr Point(int x, int y) : m_x{x}, m_y{y} { }
    constexpr int getX() const { return m_x; }
    constexpr int getY() const { return m_y; }
};
cards.explanation: Private data, constexpr constructor with initializer list, const getters, supports compile-time evaluation.

cards.front: What is the recommended order for class members?
cards.back:
1. Public members first (interface)
2. Private members second (implementation)
Within each section:
- Constructors
- Destructors
- Other member functions
- Data members last

cards.front: What are the key differences between class and struct in C++?
cards.back:
Only one difference: default access level.
  • class: members are private by default
  • struct: members are public by default
Otherwise completely equivalent. Use struct for passive data, class for objects with behaviors and invariants.

cards.concept: The complete checklist for a well-designed class
cards.explanation:
A well-designed class should:
  • Make all data members private (encapsulation)
  • Provide constructors to ensure valid initialization
  • Use member initializer lists in constructors
  • Make non-modifying member functions const
  • Make single-argument constructors explicit (unless conversion is intended)
  • Provide only necessary getters/setters
  • Return expensive types by const reference from getters
  • Pass/return class objects by const reference when possible
  • Use meaningful names with m_ prefix for members
examples: public: explicit Fraction(int n, int d=1);
